summary:
The primary cause of fetal congenital heart block due to maternal immune-mediated effects is maternal anti-Ro/SSA or anti-La/SSB antibodies leading to neonatal lupus. Therefore, women who have had fetuses affected by congenital heart block must undergo screening for antiphospholipid antibodies—specifically those containing anti-Ro/SSA and/or anti-La/SSB—to identify risk early.

Okay, let me now reframe based strictly upon only clinically essential facts:

Fetal congenital heart block results primarily from transplacental passage of maternal anti-Ro/SSA or anti-La/SSB antibodies causing neonatal lupus. Thus, the appropriate maternal screening involves detecting these same antibodies through standard antiphospholipid antibody panel testing.

Final summary after filtering all non-clinical details down to just two concise statements focusing solely on direct medical relevance:

Maternal anti-Ro/SSA or anti-La/SSB antibodies are responsible for most cases of fetal congenital heart block via neonatal lupus. Mothers should thus be tested using an antiphospholipid antibody panel including both anti-Ro and anti-La assays.
